content.full_text:
Farn Porrrrcal PnecrrcEs ColrnrrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95g14-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0986
Novernber 10,2004
H. Peter Klein
Office of the County Counsel
501 Low Gap Road, Room 1030
Ukiah, CA 95482
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 4-04-180
Dear Mr. Klein:
This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Planning Commissioner
Karen Calvert for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisionr oftn" political
Reform Act (the "Act").I
QUESTION
May Mendocino County Planning Commissioner Karen Calvert take part in
planning commission deliberations and voting on a proposed grading ordinance?
CONCLUSION
Yes. A possible, relatively modest effect on the cost of building a driveway
would not create a conflict of interest disqualifring Commissioner Calvert from taking
part in decisions on the proposed grading ordinance.
FACTS
Karen Calvert is a member of the planning commission and either she or her son
is likely to build a house on a property not zoned for timber production within the next
ten years. However, such construction would require a driveway that is likely to trigger a
grading ordinance (see Klein, Advice Letter No. I-04-038), whiih is currently before the
Mendocino County Planning Commission. The grading ordinance * propor"d could
change the cost of such a driveway by more than $500.
I Govemment Code sectiors 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of,the California Code of Regulations.


FileNo. ,{-04-180
Page No. 2
ANALYSIS
Your present request for advice is, in effect, a follow-up to our earlier letter
discussing the Act's conflict of interest provisions as they would apply to all mernbers
of the planning commission who make decisions regarding the proposed new grading
ordinance, described at some length in ourprior letter. Your inquiry now is more narrow,
concerning only Commissioner Calvert and her economic interest in one parcel of real
property.' We had earlier concluded that all members of the planning commission were
public officials within the meaning of section 82048,who would be making, participating
in making or using their official positions to influence governmental decisions regarding
the proposed ordinance. To anslser your current question, we begin by considering
whether Commissioner Calvert's economic interests are directly or indirectly involved in
these governmental decisions, taking up the analysis at step 4 ofthe 8-step analysis
employed in our prior letter.
Step 4. Are Commissioner Calvert's Economic fnturests Directty or Indirectly fnvolved
in Decisions on the Proposed Grading Ordinance?
We found in our earlier letter that the Commissioners' real property interests are
only indirectly involved in the decisions at issug because the scope *t "ff""t of the
proposed grading ordinance falls under the provisions of regulation 187O4.2(b)- as an
amendment of a land use regulation applicable to all properties with specified'
characteristics throughout the unincorporated areas of the county.
Commissioner Calvert,like all public officials, has an economic interest in her
personal finances, and a foreseeable increase or decrease of $250 or more in her income
or expenses in any l2-month period would ordinarily constitute amaterial financial effect
on this economic interest in herpersonal finances. (Regulation 18705.5(a).) However,
this regulation also provides that in evaluating personal financial effectq we may not
consider financial effects on the value ofreal property owned by the official. What this
means, as a practical matter, is that "an effect on real estate... is always considered if
present in place of a personal financial effect." (Hunt Advice Letter, No. 4-02-073.)
Accordingly, we proceed with our analysis by considering only financial effects on
Commissioner Calvert's real property, which is indirectly involved in decisions on the
ordinance.
2 You report that either Commissioner Calvert or her son may build a house on this parcel ofreal property.
We are not told that Commissioner Calvert's son is a public official, and will presgme that he is not. Since
the Act's conflict of interest provisions would not apply to him in that case, we will not refer to
Commissioner Calvert's son in the following analysis. We are also not told who owns the proposed
building site, and will presume that Commissioner Calvert has at least a legal ownership lnierest in tnis
property worth $2,000 or more. Thus the following analysis presumes thaiCommissioner Calvert may
build a home on the properfy in question, in which she has anownership interest sufficient to implicati the
Act's conllict of interest rules.


FileNo. A-04-180
Page No. 3
Steps 5 & 6. fs ft Reasonably Foreseeable that the Changed Cost of a Driveway Would
have a Material Financial Effect on Commissioner Calvert's Real Properfir fnturest?
Having identified her economic interest and determined that the real property is
indirectly involved in the decision at issue, we must next identiff the materiality standard
appropriate to Commissioner Calvert's real property interest. Regulation t8705.2(b)
provides that financial effects on real property indirectly involved in a governmental
decision are presumed not to be material. However:
'"This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are
specifi c circumstances regarding the governmental
decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real
property in which the public official has an economic
interest, which make it reasonabry foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on the real
property in which the public official has an interest.
Examples of specific circumstances that will be considered
include, but are not limited to, circumstances where the
decision affects:
(A) The development potential or income producing
potential of the real property in which the officiar has an
economic interest;
(B) The use of the real property in which the officiar has an
economic interest;
(C) The character of the neighborhood including but not
limited to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy
intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits
ofthe neighborhood." (Id.)
If at the time a decision is made an official neither knows norhas reason to know
facts that would rebut the presumption of non-materiality, the official may pres,me that
any financial effects on his or her indirectly involved real property will nitbematerial.
Commissioner Calvert does, however, indicate that if she decides to build a house on the
property, the proposed grading ordinance could change the cost of building a driveway by
an amount in excess of $500. From what we know at prese,nt, the only fact ttrat rnighi
rebut the presumption of non-materialityis the changd cost ofbuilding this driveway.
Such an effect does not appear to be the kind of circrmrstance illustated by the "*u*plo
given in regulation 18705.20), which refer to the use and value of a property as a whole.
Specific monetarythresholds no longer establish materialityunderregulation
18705.2(b). Prior to its amendment in late 2}N,this regulation did state a *utoiutity
threshold for effects of $I0,000 or more on the fair martet value of the property. It does
not appear that material ef;fects on real property owned by a public official were ever
defined in terms of increased construction costs in the range of a few hundred or a
thousand dollars, but focused instead on larger effects on the value of the property as a
whole. Commissioner Calvert does not suggest that a driveway could not beiuilt at all,


FileNo. A-04-180
Page No. 4
which might substantially alter the use, value, or development potential of the property.
A relatively modest increase in the cost of putting in a driveway, should she decidl to
build a house on the property, is not a circumstance competant to rebut the regulatory
presumption that any financial effects on this real property interest are not *at"rial.
Because we conclude that the changed cost of a driveway on Commissioner
Calvert's real property would not amotrnt to a "material financial effect" as defined at
regulation 18705.2(b), and because we have no information on other circumstances that
might rebut the presumption of non-materiality we can only conclude that a "material
financial effect" is not a reasonably foreseeable outcome of decisions on the proposed
grading ordinance.
Steps 7 and 8: Exceptions
An official who othe,nrise would have a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate in that decision ifthe circumstances are such that the'lublic generally', rule
might be invoked. This rule applies when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision upon apublic official's economic interests is'tnaterial,"but not distinguishable
from foreseeable effects on a significant segment of thepublic generally. (Section 87103;
regulation 18707(a).) Because we do not find a foreseeable'tnaterial" financial effect,
the public generally rule has no application in this case.
The same is true for the "legally required participation" rule codified at section
87101 and regulation 18708. This is an exception that typically applies when an agency
is unable to assemble a quonrm of its members without theparticipation of an omCiA 
-
who has a conflict of interest. At presen! we have no reason to believe that
Commissioner Calvert or a quonrm of the planning commission have disquali&ing
conflicts of interest and are thus unavailable to make legally required desisions on the
draft ordinance.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact rne at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
Lrwjg

QUESTION: May Mendocino County Planning Commissioner Karen Calvert take part in
planning commission deliberations and voting on a proposed grading ordinance?

CONCLUSION: Yes. A possible, relatively modest effect on the cost of building a driveway
would not create a conflict of interest disqualifring Commissioner Calvert from taking
part in decisions on the proposed grading ordinance.
